learner.title: The River Scholar
learner.message: You love to understand the 'why' and 'how'. You believe that before we can save our rivers, we must truly understand the science, the sources, and the systems behind them.
learner.summary: Your strengths lie in learning, synthesizing information, and educating others.

volunteer.title: The Ground Warrior
volunteer.message: You are a person of action. You don't just want to read about the river; you want to be on the banks with gloves on, making a physical difference today.
volunteer.summary: Your strengths lie in community organizing, field work, and immediate impact.

explorer.title: The Basin Mapper
explorer.message: You see the big picture. You love tracing a river from its origin to its mouth, understanding its geography, and cataloging its health and tributaries.
explorer.summary: Your strengths lie in documentation, reference building, and geographical tracking.

researcher.title: The Data Sentinel
researcher.message: You trust what you can measure. You look for empirical truth through datasets, satellite imagery, water quality indices, and rigorous scientific methodology.
researcher.summary: Your strengths lie in analysis, academic rigor, and evidence-based planning.

devotee.title: The Cultural Guardian
devotee.message: You view the river as a living, sacred entity. You are drawn to the ancient scriptures, the rituals, and the deep civilisational history that ties our culture to the water.
devotee.summary: Your strengths lie in preserving traditions, translating texts, and honoring heritage.

thinker.title: The Community Weaver
thinker.message: You know that conversation sparks change. You love connecting ideas, summarizing discussions, and bringing people together to share bite-sized insights.
thinker.summary: Your strengths lie in communication, curation, and social engagement.

questions.text: When you visit a river, what is the very first thing that captures your attention?

options.text: The physical state of the banks and the waste left behind.
The water quality metrics, flow rate, and ecological health.
The temples, the ghats, and the people performing rituals.
The community living around it and what they are discussing.
The geographical flow, the landscape, and where it travels next.
The scientific reasons why the river looks the way it does.

questions.text: How do you naturally prefer to process new, complex information?

options.text: By learning a practical, step-by-step protocol I can immediately apply.
By reading structured, deeply explained educational articles.
By looking at raw data, charts, methodologies, and scientific papers.
By listening to historical stories, ancient texts, and traditions.
By viewing encyclopedias, interactive maps, and geographical facts.
By having open discussions and sharing bite-sized thoughts with others.

questions.text: If you had a completely free weekend to dedicate to a river, what would you do?

options.text: Join a local group to pick up plastic and document waste on the banks.
Start a multi-part online course about river basin hydrology.
Dive into a complex dataset mapping pollution hotspots over 10 years.
Visit a historic ghat to understand the local traditions and history.
Trace the route of a major river zone on a map to learn its tributaries.
Start a forum thread asking people how their local river has changed.

questions.text: If you were to write a book or direct a documentary about rivers, what would the title be?

options.text: 'The Science of Water: Understanding Pollution at the Source'
'Grassroots Heroes: Real Action on the Riverbanks'
'Atlas of the Great Indian Basins: A Geographical Journey'
'Data and Discharge: Advanced Hydrological Trends'
'Sacred Waters: Myths, Legends, and Living Traditions'
'Voices of the River: Communities and Conversations'

questions.text: When you are browsing social media or the internet, what kind of river content are you most likely to share?

options.text: A detailed thread breaking down a complex environmental concept.
Photos or calls-to-action for an upcoming local cleanup drive.
A highly detailed, beautifully color-coded map of a river system.
A link to a newly published, peer-reviewed study on water quality.
A beautiful verse, stotra, or historical fact about a sacred river.
A thought-provoking question asking followers for their opinions.

questions.text: If you were to contribute directly to a Wiki like Nadikosh, what task sounds the most fun?

options.text: Writing a 5-part explainer series for absolute beginners.
Creating a step-by-step checklist toolkit for NGO workers.
Updating the general profile, stats, and overview of a specific tributary.
Reviewing and annotating a government dataset on heavy metals.
Translating an ancient Sanskrit text detailing a specific river ritual.
Curating the best facts and forum debates into bite-sized summaries.

questions.quote: Getting to the root of the issue.
questions.text: What frustrates you the *most* about the current state of river conservation?

options.text: The lack of accessible, easy-to-understand education for the general public.
People talking endlessly on the internet but not doing anything on the ground.
How fragmented, scattered, and hard to find basic river information is.
Policies and decisions being made without rigorous, accurate data backing them.
The loss of spiritual reverence, respect, and traditional connection to the water.
The fact that conversations are isolated and different groups aren't connecting.

questions.text: When someone mentions 'The Ganga', what is the first image that pops into your head?

options.text: The biochemical breakdown of its self-purifying properties.
Volunteers pulling nets of plastic from the Varanasi ghats.
A map showing its massive 2,500+ km journey from Gangotri to the Bay of Bengal.
A line graph showing fluctuating Dissolved Oxygen levels over the last decade.
The evening Aarti, the ringing of bells, and floating diyas.
Millions of people whose daily lives, economies, and stories depend on it.

questions.text: If you had to convince a local politician to protect a river, what would be your main weapon?

options.text: A well-structured presentation explaining the root causes of the problem.
A petition signed by thousands of angry, mobilized local citizens.
A comprehensive report showing all the towns and economies affected downstream.
Hard, undeniable, peer-reviewed data proving toxic contamination.
Reminding them of their moral, cultural, and historical duty to our ancestors.
A viral social media campaign summarizing the public's outrage.

questions.text: At the deepest level, how do you view a river?

options.text: A complex ecological system waiting to be understood and taught.
A shared physical resource that requires our immediate, hands-on protection.
A geographical lifeline that connects regions, climates, and topographies.
A dynamic set of measurable hydrological and chemical variables.
A divine mother, a tirtha, and a living spiritual entity.
A focal point for human society, culture, debate, and connection.
